ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for roti prata or coin prata. FOODON_00002682 (flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Flat bread) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for roti prata or coin prata with fish accompaniment.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for coin prata or roti prata as a specific food concept.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 3–4 coin pratas with fish curry)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of coin prata with fish consists of approximately 3 to 4 small coin-sized pratas (each roughly 30–35 g) accompanied by a fish curry or fish gravy portion, totalling approximately 220 g.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Prata (plain, cooked) and Fish Curry — HPB Singapore Foodo / ICMR-NIN nutrient composition data, adapted for coin prata with fish serving
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Coin prata is a smaller, coin-sized variant of plain roti prata. Nutrient values per 100 g are adapted from HPB Foodo data for plain roti prata (cooked) combined with a proportional fish curry component based on ICMR-NIN fish curry data. The combined per-100 g values reflect an approximate 65% prata / 35% fish curry ratio by weight, consistent with a typical coin prata with fish serving. Energy and macros are cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy data for plain paratha/flatbread to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Indian flatbreads and paratha — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coin prata with fish. GI of 57 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain paratha/roti (GI approximately 55–62, mean ~57) as the closest analogous Indian flatbread. The fish curry accompaniment contributes protein and fat which may modestly lower the effective GI of the meal; however, the GI reported here reflects the prata component as the primary glycemic driver. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 57.2) / 100 ≈ 32.5, where carbohydrate per 220 g serving ≈ 57.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~682 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit coin prata with fish to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt fish curry preparation, as a single serving can contribute approximately 30% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per serving (~32.5)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume coin prata with fish in moderation; the refined wheat flour base produces a medium GI response and a high glycemic load per full serving, and portion control to 1–2 coin pratas with fish curry is advisable to limit postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~4.1 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit coin prata with fish due to its saturated fat content from ghee used in preparation, and should favour fish curry preparations using lean fish and minimal oil to reduce overall saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coin prata with fish is a popular Singaporean-Indian hawker dish consisting of small, coin-sized roti prata flatbreads served with a fish curry or fish gravy accompaniment. Roti prata is a South Indian-origin layered flatbread made from refined wheat flour, ghee, egg, water, and salt. The dough is stretched, folded repeatedly to create flaky layers, and then cooked on a flat griddle with ghee or oil until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft and chewy inside. The 'coin' variant refers to the smaller, palm-sized portions compared to standard prata, making it a popular snack or light meal option. The fish accompaniment is typically a spiced fish curry made with onions, tomatoes, coconut milk or tamarind, and a blend of Indian spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chilli. Nutritionally, coin prata with fish provides moderate energy (~218 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from both the egg in the prata and the fish (~8.2 g per 100 g), and carbohydrates primarily from the refined wheat flour base (~26 g per 100 g). The dish has a medium glycemic index (~57) driven by the refined flour, and a high glycemic load per full serving due to portion size. Saturated fat from ghee is a consideration for cardiovascular health. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on the fish curry preparation. Coin prata with fish is widely available at Indian hawker stalls and mamak restaurants across Singapore and is commonly consumed as breakfast, supper, or a snack.
common_user_queries: How many calories in coin prata with fish?
Is coin prata with fish healthy?
Is prata with fish high in carbs?
Coin prata with fish calories Singapore
What is the difference between coin prata and regular prata?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists specifically for coin prata with fish as a combined dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo plain roti prata data combined with ICMR-NIN fish curry data in an estimated 65:35 ratio by weight. GI is estimated from the closest analogous flatbread in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source databases for this food category. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases used. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases used. 4. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the adapted source data. 5. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database paratha/roti data; no direct GI measurement for coin prata with fish exists in the literature. 6. The fish component is assumed to be a standard spiced fish curry; actual nutrient values will vary depending on fish species, curry recipe, and oil quantity used by individual hawker stalls. 7. Coin prata is typically halal at Muslim-operated hawker stalls; halal status should be verified per vendor. 8. The GI category is 'medium' as GI = 57, which falls in the 56–69 range per Veritas threshold rules.
